items.q: What is the difference between a silent and a missense mutation?
items.correct: A silent mutation changes a base but the codon still codes for the same amino acid; a missense mutation changes the amino acid encoded.
items.misconception: A silent mutation has no effect because no base actually changes.
items.random_wrong: Missense and silent mutations always produce a non-functional protein.
items.why: Students think 'silent' means 'no change at the DNA level' rather than 'no change at the protein level' (due to genetic code degeneracy).

items.q: During an action potential, do Na+ and K+ move through the same channels?
items.correct: No — voltage-gated Na+ channels open first (depolarisation, Na+ in), then voltage-gated K+ channels open (repolarisation, K+ out).
items.misconception: Na+ and K+ swap places through the same channel at the same time.
items.random_wrong: Only Na+ moves during an action potential; K+ stays still.
items.why: Students simplify ion movement as 'they swap' rather than recognising two separate channel types opening sequentially.

items.q: What is the role of DNA ligase when inserting a gene into a plasmid?
items.correct: It forms phosphodiester bonds that join the gene to the cut plasmid, sealing the recombinant DNA.
items.misconception: DNA ligase cuts the plasmid open so the gene can be inserted.
items.random_wrong: DNA ligase reads the gene and translates it into protein.
items.why: Students confuse the cutting enzymes (restriction endonucleases) with the joining enzyme (ligase); both are needed but do opposite jobs.

items.q: Which ion triggers neurotransmitter release at the presynaptic terminal?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ca²⁺. “Na⁺” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Balance and coordination are mainly controlled by:
items.why: It's Cerebellum. “Cerebrum” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If each EPSP is 1 mV, how many simultaneous EPSPs would be needed to depolarise from -70 mV to threshold -55 mV?
items.why: The real figure is 15. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
